elements.sent-2411-001: The NTK is a widely used tool in the theoretical analysis of deep learning, allowing us to look at supervised deep neural networks through the lenses of kernel regression.
elements.sent-2411-002: Recently, several works have investigated kernel models for self-supervised learning, hypothesizing that these also shed light on the behaviour of wide neural networks by virtue of the NTK.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, it remains an open question to what extent this connection is mathematically sound --- it is a commonly encountered misbelief that the kernel behaviour of wide neural networks emerges irrespective of the loss function it is trained on.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we bridge the gap between the NTK and self-supervised learning, focusing on two-layer neural networks trained under the Barlow Twins loss.
elements.sent-2411-005: We prove that the NTK of Barlow Twins indeed becomes constant as the width of the network approaches infinity.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our analysis technique is different from previous works on the NTK and may be of independent interest.
elements.sent-2411-007: Overall, our work provides a first rigorous justification for the use of classic kernel theory to understand self-supervised learning of wide neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-008: Building on this result, we derive generalization error bounds for kernelized Barlow Twins and connect them to neural networks of finite width.
elements.sent-2411-009: In recent years, self-supervised learning (SSL) has emerged as a powerful paradigm, building the foundation of several modern machine learning models.
elements.sent-2411-010: At its core, SSL relies on the idea of using augmentations to encode a notion of similarity in otherwise unlabeled data.
elements.sent-2411-011: As a typical example, consider an image dataset.
elements.sent-2411-012: Even though labels may not be known, it is reasonable to believe that randomly cropping, slightly rotating, or blurring the images will not change the true underlying class information.
elements.sent-2411-013: Therefore, two augmented versions $(x,x^+)$ of the same image should receive similar representations $f(x), f(x^+)$ in a lower-dimensional ambient space.
elements.sent-2411-014: This constitutes the basic intuition behind non-contrastive SSL, and several loss functions that capture this idea have emerged.\footnote{In contrastive SSL, one additionally incorporates a notion of dissimilarity into the model by including negative examples $x^-$ into the training procedure.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Among the most popular losses is Barlow Twins \citep{zbontar2021barlow , a loss function that pushes the cross-correlation of the embeddings $f(x), f(x^+)$ towards the identity matrix.}
elements.sent-2411-016: This aims to prevent a phenomenon known as dimension collapse, where the learned representations collapse to a single point in the embedding space.
elements.sent-2411-017: Despite the empirical success of SSL on a range of tasks \citep{radford2021learning, bachman2019learning , it has taken quite some time for deep learning theory to catch up with this innovation.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Arguably one of the most promising avenues towards understanding the fundamental principles of SSL is by connecting it to kernel methods \citep{smola1998learning .}
elements.sent-2411-019: This of course is reminiscent of the supervised setting, where the NTK \citep{jacot2018neural, lee2019wide provides a powerful framework to understand several phenomena of deep learning with wide neural networks, including generalization \citep{simon2021eigenlearning , benign overfitting \citep{mallinar2022benign , and robustness \citep{bombari2023beyond .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: While a significant number of researchers are currently looking at SSL from a kernel perspective (for an overview, see related works), and while almost all of these works are motivated with the NTK, the connection is left implicit.
elements.sent-2411-021: However, it is not a priori clear that neural networks trained under SSL actually behave like kernel machines in the infinite width limit.
elements.sent-2411-022: In fact, \citet{anil2024can have recently demonstrated that for certain contrastive loss functions, the NTK is in fact not constant at infinite width.}
elements.sent-2411-023: This casts a shadow of doubt on the validity of kernel approximations to SSL, urging us to take a closer look at the matter.
elements.sent-2411-024: In this paper, we bridge the gap between SSL and the NTK for the Barlow Twins loss.
elements.sent-2411-025: We prove that for neural networks with one hidden layer, the neural tangent kernel indeed becomes constant as the width of the network approaches infinity.
elements.sent-2411-026: The proof technique is different from previous works on the NTK, and leverages Grönwall's inequality (see Appendix \ref{app:grönwall ).}
elements.sent-2411-027: This is necessitated by the training dynamics of the Barlow Twins loss, which make an extension of existing methods difficult.
elements.sent-2411-028: Our work confirms the hypothesized connection between kernel methods and neural network based SSL, and proves that Barlow Twins is akin to one of the most prominent representation learning methods, Kernel PCA \citep{scholkopf1997kernel .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Building on these insights, we use classic tools from learning theory to derive generalization error bounds for kernel versions of Barlow Twins, and then connect them to neural networks of finite width.
elements.sent-2411-030: This paper is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-031: We discuss related works in Section \ref{sec:related and present our formal setup in Section \ref{sec:setup .}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Section \ref{sec:mainresult contains our main result, stated in the general setting of multi-dimensional embeddings $f: \R^d \rightarrow \R^K$ for the Barlow twins loss.}
elements.sent-2411-033: In Section \ref{sec:warmup we give a detailed proof sketch of the one-dimensional case, which is computationally less involved but contains the main technical ideas.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Section \ref{sec:implications derives generalization error bounds for the Barlow twins loss in an abstract Hilbert space setting.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Using the kernel trick and our newly established validity of the NTK approximation, we then relate these bounds to finite neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-036: Finally, we empirically verify our findings through experiments in Section \ref{sec:exp .}
elements.sent-2411-037: In comparison to supervised learning, the theoretical understanding of self-supervised learning is still at an early stage.
elements.sent-2411-038: Nonetheless, several works have investigated SSL using classic tools from statistical learning theory \citep{arora2019theoretical, wei2020theoretical .}
elements.sent-2411-039: Furthermore, there have been several successful attempts to look at SSL through the lenses of classic spectral and kernel methods \citep{kiani2022joint, johnson2022contrastive, haochen2021provable, cabannes2023ssl, esser2024non .}
elements.sent-2411-040: These works provide a number of useful insights into both theoretical as well as practical aspects of training SSL models, but leave the formal connection between the kernel regime and deep learning based SSL implicit.
elements.sent-2411-041: The idea that neural networks behave like (neural tangent) kernel models in the infinite width limit was first investigated by \citet{jacot2018neural .}
elements.sent-2411-042: Several later works further explored this connection in various contexts \citep{arora2019theoretical, chizat2019lazy, lee2019wide, liu2020linearity , mostly for the squared (or hinge) loss.}
elements.sent-2411-043: In particular, \cite{liu2020toward develop a general framework for looking at convergence to the NTK using the Hessian.}
elements.sent-2411-044: The Barlow Twins loss does not fall under the umbrella of previous analysis, since it is a fourth order loss with very different training dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-045: Thus, even though \citet{simon2023stepwise motivate their investigation of kernel versions of Barlow Twins precisely with the NTK, we are not aware of any derivation that proves this analogy is valid.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Closest to our work is \citet{anil2024can , who manage to bound the evolution of the NTK for certain contrastive loss functions, but do not quite prove constancy until convergence of the loss: Their bounds hold until a time that grows with network width, leaving the possibility that as wider networks are trained, the time till convergence also grows.}
elements.sent-2411-047: In this work, we analyze an idealized version of the popular Barlow Twins loss \citep{zbontar2021barlow , as considered in previous theoretical works on SSL \citep{simon2023stepwise .}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Training data consists of positive pairs, denoted $\calX = \{x_1, \dots, x_N\} \subset \R^d$ and $\calX_+ = \{x^+_1, \dots, x^+_N\} \subset \R^d$.

elements.sent-2411-051: The goal of Barlow Twins is now to learn the parameters of a neural network $f: \R^d \rightarrow \R^K$ that embeds each point into a lower-dimensional space.
elements.sent-2411-053: To encourage useful representations, the Barlow Twins loss function pushes the cross-moment matrix of $f(\calX)$ and $f(\calX_+)$ to the identity matrix in $\R^K$.

elements.paragraph-2411-001: Generalization error for Barlow Twins in Hilbert spaces.
elements.sent-2411-134: We first place ourselves in an abstract Hilbert space framework.

elements.sent-2411-183: We prove that at infinite width, the NTK of a neural network trained under the Barlow Twins loss becomes constant.
elements.sent-2411-184: This is the first result that rigorously justifies the use of traditional kernel methods to understand SSL through the lenses of the NTK.
elements.sent-2411-185: Furthermore, the kernel connection enables us to bound the population Barlow Twins loss in terms of the empirical loss and a slack term that decays with the number of samples.

elements.sent-2411-358: Recently, several works have investigated kernel models for self-supervised learning, hypothesizing that these also shed light on the behavior of wide neural networks by virtue of the NTK.
elements.sent-2411-359: However, it remains an open question to what extent this connection is mathematically sound --- it is a commonly encountered misbelief that the kernel behavior of wide neural networks emerges irrespective of the loss function it is trained on.

elements.sent-2411-361: Overall, our work provides a first justification for the use of classic kernel theory to understand self-supervised learning of wide neural networks.
